_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against ATA 2014 and 2024 Thyroid Guidelines, NICE NG145 Thyroid Disease, UpToDate, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Hypothyroidism: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hypothyroidism is a chronic endocrine disorder characterized by insufficient production of thyroid hormone by the thyroid gland, resulting in a global slowing of metabolic processes throughout the body. It is one of the most common chronic diseases in developed countries, affecting approximately 4-5% of the general population, with a prevalence that increases sharply with age and is significantly more common in women than men. The condition ranges from subclinical disease (elevated TSH with normal free T4, often asymptomatic) to overt hypothyroidism (elevated TSH with low free T4, symptomatic). The most common cause in iodine-sufficient regions is Hashimoto thyroiditis, an autoimmune condition in which T-lymphocytes progressively destroy thyroid follicular cells. Treatment with levothyroxine, a synthetic form of thyroxine (T4), is highly effective and involves lifelong hormone replacement tailored to individual TSH targets.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 4.6% of the US population (NHANES data) has overt hypothyroidism; subclinical hypothyroidism affects an additional 4-8%
keyStats.demographics: Women are 5-8 times more likely than men to develop hypothyroidism; prevalence increases with age, with 10-15% of women over 65 having elevated TSH
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed in adulthood between ages 30-50; Hashimoto thyroiditis (the leading cause) typically presents between ages 30-50, with a female-to-male ratio of approximately 7:1
keyStats.globalCases: WHO estimates approximately 360 million people worldwide have clinically significant hypothyroidism; iodine deficiency remains the leading cause globally but is rare in developed countries

definition: Hypothyroidism (ICD-10: E03.9 for unspecified hypothyroidism; E03.0 for congenital hypothyroidism; E03.1 for Hashimoto's thyroiditis; E03.2 for postablative hypothyroidism; E03.3 for postinfectious hypothyroidism; E03.4 for atrophy of thyroid) is a clinical syndrome resulting from inadequate thyroid hormone secretion, leading to slowed metabolism and reduced cellular metabolism in virtually all organ systems. Primary hypothyroidism accounts for 95% of cases and results from thyroid gland dysfunction itself. Secondary (central) hypothyroidism, much rarer, results from pituitary dysfunction (ICD-10: E03.3/E03.8), and tertiary hypothyroidism results from hypothalamic dysfunction. The clinical diagnosis is confirmed by elevated serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) with a low or inappropriately normal free thyroxine (fT4) in primary hypothyroidism. Overt hypothyroidism requires treatment; the management of subclinical hypothyroidism (elevated TSH with normal fT4) depends on TSH level, antibody status, and patient symptoms. Hypothyroidism is managed primarily by endocrinologists, with primary care physicians providing ongoing monitoring once a stable levothyroxine dose is established.

typesClassification.description: Thyroid gland dysfunction causing insufficient hormone production. Accounts for 95% of all hypothyroidism. Causes include Hashimoto thyroiditis (autoimmune), thyroidectomy, radioactive iodine therapy for hyperthyroidism, and external beam radiation. Diagnosed by elevated TSH with low free T4.

The most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient countries. An autoimmune condition where T-lymphocytes infiltrate the thyroid gland, causing progressive destruction of follicular cells and eventual fibrosis. Associated with anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies. Often presents with a goiter initially, progressing to atrophy.

Elevated serum TSH (typically 4.5-10 mIU/L) with normal free T4 levels. Often asymptomatic but may cause subtle cognitive effects, fatigue, and dyslipidemia. TSH elevation >10 mIU/L generally warrants treatment; lower elevations require individualized decisions based on age, antibody status, and symptoms.

Caused by pituitary gland dysfunction (hypopituitarism) resulting in inadequate TSH secretion. Much rarer than primary hypothyroidism. Confirmed by low or normal TSH with low free T4. Requires pituitary MRI and evaluation for other pituitary hormone deficiencies. Often associated with other pituitary hormone deficiencies.

Present from birth due to thyroid dysgenesis (absent, ectopic, or hypoplastic gland) or inborn errors of thyroid hormone synthesis. Detected by newborn screening programs within days of birth. If untreated within the first weeks of life, causes irreversible cretinism — severe developmental delay, intellectual disability, and neurological damage.

Develops after radioactive iodine (I-131) treatment for hyperthyroidism or thyroid cancer, or after surgical thyroidectomy. Typically permanent after adequate ablative doses. Requires lifelong levothyroxine replacement. TSH must be monitored every 6-8 weeks until stable after ablative therapy.

primarySymptoms: Fatigue and low energy — the most common and disabling symptom, arising from globally reduced metabolic rate and impaired mitochondrial energy production in skeletal muscle.

Cold intolerance — a hallmark symptom where patients feel unusually cold in environments others find comfortable, reflecting reduced thermogenesis and peripheral vasoconstriction from low thyroid hormone.
Weight gain — often modest (2-5 kg) despite no change in diet, reflecting reduced basal metabolic rate and impaired lipolysis. Patients also describe difficulty losing weight despite effort.
Constipation — slow gastrointestinal motility from reduced smooth muscle contractility and decreased gastrointestinal secretions, often requiring laxatives or increased fiber and fluid.

Dry, cool, coarse skin — thyroid hormone is required for normal epidermal proliferation and sebum secretion; deficiency causes xerosis (dry skin), brittle hair, and hair loss from the scalp and lateral eyebrows (madarosis).
Bradycardia — resting heart rate below 60 bpm from reduced cardiac chronotropy and reduced expression of beta-1 adrenergic receptors; may be accompanied by low-voltage ECG changes.

Cognitive slowing — impaired concentration, memory lapses, and slowed mental processing speed ('brain fog'), reflecting reduced cerebral metabolic rate. Depression is common; psychosis (myxedema madness) is rare.
Menstrual irregularities — anovulatory cycles causing oligomenorrhea or menorrhagia, and reduced fertility. Prolactin levels may be mildly elevated due to TRH stimulation, causing galactorrhea.
Myxedema — non-pitting edema from accumulation of glycosaminoglycans in skin and soft tissues, particularly around the eyes (periorbital edema), hands, and feet. Distinct from simple fluid retention.

Elevated cholesterol — increased LDL cholesterol and triglycerides from reduced hepatic LDL receptor expression and reduced lipoprotein lipase activity, increasing cardiovascular risk even in subclinical disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained persistent fatigue that doesn't improve with rest or sleep — a cardinal symptom that is often dismissed but is one of the most consistent early indicators
Feeling cold when others are comfortable, particularly in the hands and feet
New onset constipation in someone with previously regular bowel habits
Unexplained weight gain without dietary changes
Dry skin and hair changes that don't respond to topical treatments
Cognitive changes — difficulty concentrating, memory problems, slowed thinking — in an otherwise healthy adult
New menstrual irregularities or fertility difficulties
Elevated cholesterol on a routine blood test, particularly in the context of other nonspecific symptoms
emergencySigns: Myxedema coma — a life-threatening decompensation of severe hypothyroidism, presenting with profound lethargy, hypothermia (core temperature <35C), bradycardia, hypotension, and progressive obtundation. Mortality rates are 40-60% even with aggressive treatment. Precipitants include infection, cold exposure, sedatives, and myocardial infarction.

Severe hyponatremia — hypothyroidism impairs free water clearance, and when combined with elevated ADH (common in severe cases), can cause dangerous dilutional hyponatremia with seizures and altered consciousness.
Pericardial effusion — severe, long-standing hypothyroidism can cause accumulation of pericardial fluid, and rarely cardiac tamponade. An underappreciated complication of untreated disease.

Acute psychosis or severe confusion — myxedema madness is rare but presents with paranoid delusions, hallucinations, and severe cognitive impairment, often in older patients with longstanding untreated disease.
Adrenal crisis — central hypothyroidism (from pituitary disease) can mask concurrent adrenal insufficiency; starting levothyroxine without cortisol coverage can precipitate acute adrenal crisis.

causes.description: The most common cause in iodine-sufficient regions. An autoimmune process where CD8+ T-lymphocytes directly kill thyroid follicular cells, and CD4+ Th1 cells drive inflammation through IFN-gamma. Anti-TPO antibodies are present in >90% of patients; anti-thyroglobulin in 60-80%. The disease typically progresses from a euthyroid state through subclinical then overt hypothyroidism over years.

Removal of thyroid tissue reduces hormone-producing capacity. Completion hypothyroidism after partial thyroidectomy depends on the volume of remaining tissue. Permanent hypothyroidism after total thyroidectomy requires lifelong levothyroxine replacement. TSH must be monitored every 6-8 weeks post-surgery until stable.

Used to treat hyperthyroidism and thyroid cancer. The radiation damages thyroid follicular cells, progressively reducing hormone production. Hypothyroidism is the intended outcome in thyroid cancer treatment; in Graves' disease, it occurs in 80% of patients within 10 years. Latent period is typically 2-6 months after treatment.

The leading cause of hypothyroidism globally. Iodine is an essential substrate for thyroid hormone synthesis — thyroid follicular cells require approximately 60 micrograms of iodine daily. Severe deficiency causes goiter and hypothyroidism; in pregnancy, iodine deficiency causes cretinism in the fetus. Rare in developed countries due to iodized salt programs.

Amiodarone (contains high iodine content and directly toxic to thyroid cells), lithium (inhibits thyroid hormone release), interferon-alpha, tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sunitinib, sorafenib), and excess iodide (from kelp supplements, radiographic contrast) can all cause hypothyroidism by impairing hormone synthesis.

Pituitary adenomas, pituitary surgery or radiation, Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary necrosis), sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis, and infiltrative diseases of the pituitary can impair TSH secretion. Less common than primary hypothyroidism but requires different treatment approach.

Failure of thyroid gland development (agenesis, hypoplasia, ectopic thyroid) accounts for 85% of congenital hypothyroidism cases. More common in females and in infants with Down syndrome. Newborn screening has virtually eliminated the intellectual disability that once resulted from delayed diagnosis.

riskFactors.description: Women are 5-8 times more likely to develop Hashimoto's thyroiditis and hypothyroidism than men. The female predominance is thought to relate to X chromosome-linked immune regulatory genes, hormonal influences on immune function, and the immunological effects of pregnancy.

Prevalence of hypothyroidism increases progressively with age. TSH reference ranges are wider in elderly populations, and mild TSH elevation is common. Distinguishing age-related TSH rise from true hypothyroidism requires careful clinical correlation.

Hashimoto's thyroiditis has a strong genetic component. First-degree relatives of affected individuals have a 4-5 fold increased risk. Specific HLA alleles (HLA-DR3, HLA-DR4) and CTLA4, PTPN22, and thyroglobulin gene polymorphisms confer susceptibility.

Autoimmune diseases cluster together — type 1 diabetes, celiac disease, vitiligo, Addison's disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and lupus all increase the risk of developing Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Polyglandular autoimmune syndromes are well-recognized.

Postpartum thyroiditis affects 5-10% of women within the first year after delivery. An autoimmune inflammatory process causes transient hyper- or hypothyroidism, often followed by permanent hypothyroidism within 5-10 years in 20-40% of cases.

Both iodine deficiency and iodine excess can cause hypothyroidism. Chronic excess iodine (from amiodarone, kelp supplements, or high-iodine diets) inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis (Wolff-Chaikoff effect) — particularly dangerous in those with underlying Hashimoto's.

External beam radiation to the neck for Hodgkin lymphoma, head and neck cancers, or childhood acne destroys thyroid follicular cells and causes hypothyroidism in 20-50% of patients within 10 years. Lifelong TSH monitoring is required.

Cigarette smoking has a complex relationship with Hashimoto's — smoking reduces the risk of developing Graves' disease but is associated with more severe Hashimoto's disease in those who have it. Smokers with Hashimoto's are more likely to progress to hypothyroidism.

affectedDemographics: Hypothyroidism affects approximately 4.6% of the US population with overt disease and 4-8% with subclinical disease (NHANES III data)
Women are 5-8 times more likely to be affected than men, with the gender gap widening in reproductive age
Prevalence increases from approximately 2% in those aged 18-39 to 10-15% in those over 65 years old
Hashimoto's thyroiditis (the leading cause) has a peak incidence between ages 30-50, with female-to-male ratio of 7:1
Newborn screening detects congenital hypothyroidism in approximately 1 in 2,000-3,000 live births in developed countries
Autoimmune thyroid disease is the most common autoimmune condition, more common than type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, or lupus
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of primary hypothyroidism is made by measuring serum TSH and free T4. An elevated TSH (>4.5-5.0 mIU/L, depending on assay) with a low free T4 confirms overt primary hypothyroidism. Subclinical hypothyroidism is diagnosed when TSH is elevated but free T4 is normal. In primary care settings, TSH is the appropriate initial test — it is exquisitely sensitive to small changes in thyroid hormone and will be abnormal before free T4 falls. When TSH is elevated, free T4 should be measured to determine severity. Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies confirm autoimmune (Hashimoto's) etiology in most cases and predict progression from subclinical to overt hypothyroidism. Central hypothyroidism requires measurement of both TSH and free T4 (both low or normal TSH with low free T4), followed by MRI of the pituitary and evaluation for other pituitary hormone deficiencies. In elderly patients, the upper limit of normal TSH should be adjusted upward (some guidelines suggest 5-6 mIU/L in those over 80) to avoid overdiagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary diagnostic panel for hypothyroidism. TSH is the most sensitive test for primary hypothyroidism — it rises before free T4 falls. Elevated TSH + low free T4 = overt primary hypothyroidism. Elevated TSH + normal free T4 = subclinical hypothyroidism. Normal TSH = euthyroid.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A simple blood draw. TSH results are typically available within 1-2 days. TSH should be rechecked 6-8 weeks after starting or adjusting levothyroxine, as TSH takes 6-8 weeks to equilibrate after a T4 dose change.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the most common autoantibody in Hashimoto's thyroiditis — positive in >90% of patients. Helps confirm autoimmune etiology, predict progression of subclinical to overt hypothyroidism, and assess postpartum thyroiditis risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood test, often ordered alongside TSH and free T4. Positive in Hashimoto's; also positive in 10-20% of Graves' disease patients and in some healthy individuals without thyroid disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Positive in 60-80% of Hashimoto's thyroiditis patients. Less specific than anti-TPO but used as an adjunct. Also used as a tumor marker in thyroid cancer patients post-thyroidectomy — in that context, rising titers suggest cancer recurrence.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood test. Usually ordered alongside anti-TPO when autoimmune thyroiditis is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates thyroid gland size, texture, and structure. Useful for characterizing a goiter, distinguishing Hashimoto's thyroiditis (diffusely heterogeneous, hypoechoic gland) from nodular disease, and guiding biopsy of suspicious nodules.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive imaging with a probe placed on the neck. Takes 15-20 minutes. No radiation. Painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Hypothyroidism causes elevated LDL cholesterol, increased total cholesterol, and elevated triglycerides. A lipid panel helps assess cardiovascular risk in newly diagnosed hypothyroidism and establishes a baseline before levothyroxine treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fasting blood draw, typically the same sample as TSH testing. Results in 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Required when secondary (central) hypothyroidism is suspected — i.e., low or normal TSH with low free T4. Evaluates for pituitary adenoma, empty sella, infiltrative disease, or other structural causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI of the sella turcica region with contrast. Takes 30-45 minutes. May be combined with assessment of other pituitary axes (cortisol, IGF-1, prolactin, gonadotropins).

treatmentOverview: Treatment of overt primary hypothyroidism involves daily levothyroxine (synthetic T4) replacement, which is initiated at a low dose in older patients and at a full replacement dose (1.6-1.8 mcg/kg/day) in younger, otherwise healthy patients. Levothyroxine is the preferred formulation because T4 has a long half-life (7 days), producing stable serum levels with once-daily dosing. The goal is to normalize TSH to the individual target (typically 0.4-4.0 mIU/L for most adults, with higher targets for elderly patients and those with cardiovascular disease). Patients should take levothyroxine on an empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before breakfast, as food and many supplements (calcium, iron, PPIs) impair absorption. TSH should be rechecked 6-8 weeks after any dose change. Most patients require lifelong treatment. Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH <10 mIU/L with normal free T4) requires individualized treatment decisions — treatment is generally offered when TSH is >10 mIU/L, when anti-TPO antibodies are positive (indicating progressive disease), or when symptoms are attributable to hypothyroidism. Central hypothyroidism requires evaluation of adrenal function before starting levothyroxine (to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis), and treatment targets free T4 in the upper half of the reference range.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine (T4) — full replacement dose 1.6-1.8 mcg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Synthetic thyroxine, the standard of care for primary hypothyroidism. Normalizes serum T4 and suppresses TSH in most patients. Initiated at 25-50 mcg/day in patients over 65 or with cardiovascular disease, and titrated up every 6-8 weeks. In younger healthy adults, starting at full replacement dose is acceptable. Generic levothyroxine is bioequivalent but switching between formulations is discouraged due to variable bioequivalence. TSH target is typically 0.4-4.0 mIU/L, with higher targets in elderly patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Highly effective — achieves normal thyroid function in >95% of patients who are adherent and properly dosed. Overt symptoms resolve within 3-6 months of achieving euthyroid state.

medicalTreatments.name: Liothyronine (T3) — Cytomel 5-25 mcg/day in divided doses
medicalTreatments.description: Synthetic liothyronine (T3), the active thyroid hormone. Some patients with persistent symptoms despite normalized TSH may benefit from adding T3 to T4 therapy (combination therapy). The ATA recommends against routine combination therapy due to lack of consistent evidence for superior outcomes and concerns about subphysiological T4 and supra-physiological T3 peaks. May be considered in patients with persistent cognitive or mood symptoms on T4 alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest evidence for benefit in a subset of patients. The majority of RCTs show no superiority of combination T4+T3 over T4 alone. A genetic polymorphism in DIO2 (deiodinase type 2) may identify a subgroup that benefits from T3 supplementation.

medicalTreatments.name: Desiccated thyroid extract (Armour Thyroid, Nature-Throid)
medicalTreatments.description: Porcine thyroid gland extract containing both T4 and T3 in variable ratios. Not recommended by ATA guidelines as first-line therapy — concerns include variable hormone content between batches, T3 peaks that produce supraphysiological serum T3 levels, and lack of long-term safety data. Some patients prefer it and report better symptom control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Variable. Some patients report improved well-being compared to levothyroxine, but RCT evidence does not consistently support superiority. The American Thyroid Association 2014 and 2024 guidelines recommend against routine use.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose levothyroxine for subclinical hypothyroidism
medicalTreatments.description: For subclinical hypothyroidism with TSH >10 mIU/L, or TSH 4.5-10 mIU/L with positive anti-TPO antibodies or significant symptoms, low-dose levothyroxine (typically 25-50 mcg/day) is initiated and titrated based on TSH response at 6-8 week intervals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective at normalizing TSH. Evidence for symptomatic benefit in subclinical disease is mixed — younger patients and those with positive antibodies are more likely to benefit.

surgicalOptions.name: There is no surgical treatment for hypothyroidism
surgicalOptions.description: Hypothyroidism is treated medically with hormone replacement. Surgery is not a treatment for hypothyroidism — it is a cause of hypothyroidism (thyroidectomy). The only surgical consideration is when a patient has severe, refractory symptoms despite optimal medical management and has a large, symptomatic goiter causing compressive symptoms (dysphagia, stridor, cosmetic concern), in which case thyroidectomy may be considered.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary iodine optimization
alternativeTreatments.description: For iodine deficiency hypothyroidism, ensuring adequate iodine intake (150 mcg/day for adults; 220 mcg/day in pregnancy; 290 mcg/day in lactation) via iodized salt or dietary sources is essential. Iodine supplementation is not beneficial in Hashimoto's (autoimmune) hypothyroidism and may worsen autoimmune inflammation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Selenium supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Selenium (200 mcg/day selenomethionine) modestly reduces anti-TPO antibody titers in Hashimoto's thyroiditis and may reduce levothyroxine requirements. A 2021 meta-analysis showed selenium supplementation reduced anti-TPO antibodies by 20-30%. Recommended by some endocrinology societies as adjunctive therapy in autoimmune thyroiditis.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit on antibody titers; uncertain clinical significance. Should not replace levothyroxine when indicated.

alternativeTreatments.name: Treatment of associated dyslipidemia
alternativeTreatments.description: Statin therapy may be indicated in hypothyroid patients with hypercholesterolemia. Importantly, statins should not be initiated until hypothyroidism is well-controlled — LDL cholesterol often normalizes significantly once euthyroid, potentially avoiding the need for statins.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist should be seen when the diagnosis of hypothyroidism is unclear (subclinical disease with borderline TSH, central hypothyroidism, or discrepant antibody results); when levothyroxine dosing is complex (elderly patients, cardiac disease, pregnancy, pediatric patients); when there is a thyroid nodule requiring evaluation; when TSH remains abnormal despite adequate levothyroxine dosing (suggesting malabsorption, drug interaction, or non-adherence); when pituitary or hypothalamic disease is suspected; or when considering combination T4/T3 therapy or desiccated thyroid extract.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified endocrinologist with experience in thyroid disorders — not all endocrinologists subspecialize in thyroid disease. Ask whether they manage thyroid disease actively and whether they use TSH targets tailored to the individual (age, cardiac status, pregnancy). For stable, well-controlled primary hypothyroidism, a primary care physician can manage ongoing monitoring. Seek endocrinology for initial diagnosis, complex dosing (elderly, cardiac, pregnancy), persistently abnormal TSH despite treatment, thyroid nodules, or suspected central hypothyroidism.
preventionStrategies: Ensure adequate dietary iodine intake — 150 mcg/day for adults, via iodized salt, fish, dairy, or supplements in iodine-deficient regions
Screen for hypothyroidism during pregnancy — TSH should be checked in the first trimester in all pregnant women; women with existing hypothyroidism need increased levothyroxine doses during pregnancy
Avoid excess iodine supplementation if you have Hashimoto's thyroiditis — high-dose kelp, iodine supplements, and amiodarone can worsen autoimmune thyroiditis
Avoid smoking — smokers have more severe Hashimoto's disease and higher rates of progression to hypothyroidism
Treat associated autoimmune conditions — screening for celiac disease and type 1 diabetes is recommended in patients with Hashimoto's and their first-degree relatives
lifestyleModifications: Take levothyroxine consistently — same time, same conditions (empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before food), same manufacturer when possible
Separate levothyroxine from calcium, iron, magnesium, and antacids by at least 4 hours — these impair absorption significantly
Maintain consistent dietary habits — large meals and high-fiber diets can affect levothyroxine absorption
Inform all physicians of your levothyroxine dose — many medications (PPIs, sucralfate, cholestyramine, raloxifene) interact with absorption
Monitor weight, energy, and temperature sensation as indicators of thyroid control — changes may signal need for dose review

recommended: Iodine-rich foods in iodine-deficient regions — iodized salt, fish (cod, haddock), shellfish, dairy, and eggs provide adequate iodine for thyroid hormone synthesis
Selenium-rich foods (Brazil nuts, sardines, eggs — Brazil nuts contain ~68-91 mcg of selenium per nut) — selenium is essential for deiodinase enzymes that convert T4 to T3
Iron-rich foods for hypothyroid patients with concurrent iron deficiency — hypothyroidism impairs iron absorption and iron deficiency impairs thyroid hormone synthesis; red meat, spinach, lentils
Vitamin D — hypothyroidism is associated with lower vitamin D levels; supplementation to target 30-50 ng/mL is appropriate for bone health in patients on levothyroxine
Fiber — adequate fiber (25-35 g/day) for constipation, a common hypothyroid symptom; but separate high-fiber meals from levothyroxine dosing
Water — adequate hydration for constipation management and overall metabolic support
avoid: Excess iodine supplementation in Hashimoto's thyroiditis — high-dose iodine can worsen autoimmune inflammation and trigger hypothyroidism in susceptible individuals

Goitrogenic foods in large quantities raw — cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, kale, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts), soy, and cassava contain goitrogens that interfere with thyroid hormone synthesis; cooking inactivates these compounds; moderate intake is safe
Calcium and iron supplements within 4 hours of levothyroxine — these bind levothyroxine and reduce its absorption by 20-40%
High-fiber supplements and weight loss products — fiber and certain weight loss ingredients can impair levothyroxine absorption
Soy foods in large quantities — high soy intake can increase levothyroxine requirements in some patients by inducing hepatic deiodinases and increasing enterohepatic circulation of thyroid hormones

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is beneficial for hypothyroid patients, though it does not replace levothyroxine therapy. Regular aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week of moderate intensity) improves cardiovascular health, helps manage the weight gain associated with hypothyroidism, reduces fatigue, and improves mood. Resistance training is also important for maintaining muscle mass, as hypothyroidism causes myopathy. Patients with severe, untreated hypothyroidism should start gradually and avoid intense exercise until thyroid function is normalized, as they have reduced exercise capacity and increased risk of cardiovascular strain. Once euthyroid, there are no restrictions on exercise. Swimming, cycling, walking, and strength training are all appropriate. Exercise tolerance typically improves significantly within 3-6 months of achieving euthyroid state.
dailyManagement: Take levothyroxine at the same time every morning, 30-60 minutes before breakfast, with a full glass of water
Do not take levothyroxine with calcium, iron, magnesium, multivitamins, or antacids — separate by at least 4 hours
Get TSH tested 6-8 weeks after any dose change — do not adjust dose based on symptoms alone, as symptoms lag behind TSH normalization by weeks
Inform your doctor of any new medications, supplements, or dietary changes — these can affect levothyroxine absorption and requirements
Store levothyroxine properly — away from heat and moisture; do not use tablets that appear discolored or have been stored improperly
prognosis: With appropriate levothyroxine replacement, the prognosis for primary hypothyroidism is excellent — patients achieve normal thyroid function, life expectancy is normal, and quality of life is restored to that of the general population. The key to optimal outcomes is individualized dosing targeting the appropriate TSH range for the patient's age and comorbidities, and consistent adherence to daily medication. Patients with Hashimoto's thyroiditis may have fluctuating levothyroxine requirements over time, particularly in the initial years of treatment as the autoimmune process continues to destroy thyroid tissue. Elderly patients and those with long-standing severe hypothyroidism may have residual symptoms (particularly cognitive effects) despite normalized TSH. Subclinical hypothyroidism with TSH <10 mIU/L and normal antibodies may resolve spontaneously in 20-40% of cases over 2-5 years; treatment is offered based on symptom burden and cardiovascular risk.
recoveryTimeline: With levothyroxine replacement: initial symptom improvement (energy, mood, cognition) begins within 2-4 weeks. Full resolution of overt hypothyroid symptoms typically requires 3-6 months, as peripheral tissues slowly normalize their metabolic state. Lipid levels normalize within 4-6 weeks of achieving euthyroid state. TSH takes 6-8 weeks to equilibrate after any dose change — premature TSH testing leads to inappropriate dose adjustments. Once stable on an appropriate dose, TSH is checked every 6-12 months.
complications: Cardiovascular disease — untreated hypothyroidism raises LDL cholesterol, causes diastolic hypertension and diastolic dysfunction, and is associated with increased cardiovascular morbidity and mortality; this risk is reduced but not eliminated with levothyroxine treatment

Infertility and pregnancy complications — hypothyroidism impairs ovulation and fertility; in pregnancy, untreated hypothyroidism is associated with increased risk of miscarriage, preeclampsia, preterm delivery, and impaired fetal neurodevelopment
Myxedema coma — rare but life-threatening complication of severe, long-standing untreated hypothyroidism with metabolic decompensation; mortality 40-60% even with treatment
Peripheral neuropathy — long-standing hypothyroidism can cause carpal tunnel syndrome (due to tissue edema), peripheral sensorimotor neuropathy, and myopathy
Obstructive sleep apnea — hypothyroidism reduces upper airway muscle tone and causes soft tissue swelling, worsening or unmasking sleep apnea; often improves with levothyroxine treatment
Psychiatric complications — depression is common and may be severe; anxiety, cognitive impairment, and in rare cases psychosis (myxedema madness) occur with longstanding disease

supportResources.name: American Thyroid Association (ATA) — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Professional society providing evidence-based patient information on all thyroid conditions including hypothyroidism, treatment guidelines, and a physician finder.

supportResources.name: British Thyroid Foundation
supportResources.description: UK-based charity providing support, information, and resources for patients with thyroid disorders including hypothyroidism and Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

supportResources.name: ATA 2014 Guidelines on Hypothyroidism Treatment. Jonklaas J, et al. Thyroid. 2014.
supportResources.description: The foundational ATA clinical practice guideline on hypothyroidism treatment — the most widely referenced guideline in this area.

supportResources.name: NICE NG145: Thyroid Disease Assessment and Management. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2019.
supportResources.description: The UK clinical guideline for thyroid disease including hypothyroidism — freely accessible and evidence-based.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyperthyroidism is the opposite — an overactive thyroid producing excessive thyroid hormone. Symptoms include weight loss, heat intolerance, tachycardia, tremor, and anxiety. Hypothyroidism produces the inverse: weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, bradycardia, and fatigue. Both can present with thyroid enlargement and menstrual changes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression shares fatigue, low energy, cognitive slowing, and weight changes with hypothyroidism. The distinction is made by thyroid function testing — depression does not elevate TSH or lower free T4. Many patients have both conditions, which must be treated simultaneously.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause profound fatigue, but CFS does not have abnormal thyroid function tests, does not cause cold intolerance or bradycardia, and does not respond to levothyroxine. The overlap in symptoms (fatigue, cognitive impairment) means thyroid testing is appropriate in all CFS workups.

confusedWithConditions.name: Addison's Disease (Adrenal Insufficiency)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause fatigue, hyperpigmentation (opposite in hypothyroidism — pale skin), weight loss, and nausea. Addison's causes hyperpigmentation, hypotension, and salt craving; hypothyroidism causes cold intolerance, constipation, and weight gain. The distinction is clear on cortisol and TSH testing.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism (including Graves' Disease)
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposite endocrine disorder — excess thyroid hormone. Some patients swing between hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism (postpartum thyroiditis, thyroiditis following radioactive iodine or surgery).

relatedConditions.name: Thyroid Nodules
relatedConditions.relevance: Nodules are common in Hashimoto's thyroiditis and should be monitored with ultrasound; a small percentage are malignant and require FNA biopsy.

relatedConditions.name: Postpartum Thyroiditis
relatedConditions.relevance: An autoimmune inflammatory thyroid condition occurring within 12 months of delivery; typically causes transient hyperthyroidism followed by hypothyroidism, often progressing to permanent hypothyroidism.

relatedConditions.name: Congenital Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Thyroid deficiency from birth; detected by newborn screening to prevent intellectual disability from delayed treatment.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hypothyroidism and Hashimoto's disease?
faqs.answer: Hypothyroidism is the clinical state of thyroid hormone deficiency — it is a condition, not a specific cause. Hashimoto's disease (chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis) is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in developed countries. Hashimoto's is an autoimmune condition where the immune system attacks and destroys the thyroid gland, eventually causing insufficient hormone production. Think of Hashimoto's as the specific diagnosis that leads to hypothyroidism as the consequence.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism)?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include fatigue and low energy, feeling cold when others are comfortable, unexplained weight gain, constipation, dry skin and brittle hair, slow heart rate (bradycardia), difficulty concentrating and memory problems ('brain fog'), menstrual irregularities and fertility difficulties, muscle weakness and aches, and elevated cholesterol. Many symptoms develop insidiously over months to years and are attributed to aging, stress, or lifestyle, which is why diagnosis is often delayed.

faqs.question: How is hypothyroidism diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Hypothyroidism is diagnosed with a simple blood test measuring TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) and free T4. TSH is the most sensitive test — it rises before free T4 falls, catching the disease early. An elevated TSH with a low free T4 confirms overt hypothyroidism. Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies confirm autoimmune (Hashimoto's) cause. A normal TSH rules out primary hypothyroidism in most cases.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for hypothyroidism?
faqs.answer: Levothyroxine (synthetic T4, brand names Synthroid, Levoxyl, Tirosint) is the standard of care — it is identical to the T4 your body naturally produces. Taken once daily on an empty stomach (30-60 minutes before breakfast), it restores normal thyroid hormone levels in >95% of patients. The dose is individualized based on weight (1.6-1.8 mcg/kg/day for most adults) and is adjusted by measuring TSH every 6-8 weeks until stable. Most people need it for life.

faqs.question: Will I need to take thyroid medication for life?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes — hypothyroidism is typically a permanent condition, particularly when caused by Hashimoto's thyroiditis (the gland is progressively destroyed), thyroidectomy, or radioactive iodine treatment. Lifelong treatment with levothyroxine is safe and highly effective. The only exceptions are transient forms — postpartum thyroiditis and subacute thyroiditis can cause temporary hypothyroidism that resolves. Your endocrinologist will determine if your hypothyroidism is permanent through ongoing monitoring.

faqs.question: How long does it take for levothyroxine to work?
faqs.answer: Levothyroxine starts working within days — TSH begins to fall within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment. However, full symptom resolution takes 3-6 months, as peripheral tissues slowly normalize their metabolic state. TSH should be rechecked 6-8 weeks after any dose change — checking it sooner gives misleading results because TSH takes that long to equilibrate. During the first few months, dose adjustments are common and expected.

faqs.question: Can hypothyroidism cause weight gain, and will treatment help me lose weight?
faqs.answer: Hypothyroidism causes a modest weight gain (typically 2-5 kg) through reduced basal metabolic rate. The weight gain is primarily fluid and fat, and is partly reversible with treatment. Once thyroid function is normalized, metabolic rate returns to normal, but weight loss still requires caloric deficit — thyroid hormone replacement is not a weight loss medication. Most patients do not return to their pre-hypothyroid weight without intentional diet and exercise. The metabolic benefit of treatment is real but modest.

faqs.question: Can I take levothyroxine with food, coffee, or supplements?
faqs.answer: Levothyroxine should be taken on an empty stomach with water only, 30-60 minutes before breakfast. Coffee (even black), calcium-fortified foods, calcium supplements, iron supplements, magnesium, fiber supplements, and antacids all impair levothyroxine absorption by 20-40% and should be separated by at least 4 hours. Soy in large quantities can increase levothyroxine requirements. PPIs and sucralfate also reduce absorption. Consistency in how and when you take your dose is critical for stable TSH control.

faqs.question: Is hypothyroidism linked to other autoimmune diseases?
faqs.answer: Yes — autoimmune diseases cluster together. Patients with Hashimoto's thyroiditis are at significantly increased risk of developing other autoimmune conditions including type 1 diabetes, celiac disease, vitiligo, pernicious anemia (B12 deficiency), Addison's disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and lupus. Screening for associated conditions is recommended, particularly in patients with multiple autoimmune features or a family history of autoimmune disease.

faqs.question: Can hypothyroidism affect fertility and pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes — hypothyroidism impairs ovulation (causing irregular or absent periods), reduces fertility, and increases miscarriage risk. Untreated hypothyroidism in the first trimester of pregnancy can impair fetal brain development, as the fetus depends entirely on maternal thyroid hormone during early pregnancy before its own thyroid develops (weeks 11-14). TSH should be <2.5 mIU/L before attempting conception and during the first trimester. Levothyroxine requirements increase by 30-50% during pregnancy and must be monitored every 4-6 weeks.

faqs.question: Does subclinical hypothyroidism need treatment?
faqs.answer: It depends. Subclinical hypothyroidism is defined as elevated TSH (typically 4.5-10 mIU/L) with normal free T4. Treatment is generally recommended when TSH is >10 mIU/L, when anti-TPO antibodies are positive (indicating progressive disease likely to become overt), when there are significant symptoms attributable to hypothyroidism, or in patients under 65 with elevated cardiovascular risk. When TSH is <10 with negative antibodies and no symptoms, watchful waiting with annual TSH monitoring is a reasonable option.

faqs.question: Can stress cause or worsen hypothyroidism?
faqs.answer: Chronic stress does not directly cause hypothyroidism, but it can worsen symptoms and affect thyroid hormone metabolism. Prolonged stress elevates cortisol, which suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis and reduces conversion of T4 to active T3, producing a functional hypothyroid state that can mimic or amplify primary hypothyroidism. Stress management (sleep, exercise, psychological support) improves symptoms but does not replace levothyroxine when it is indicated.

metaDescription: Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid): causes, symptoms, diagnosis, and levothyroxine treatment. Expert-reviewed guide from endocrinologists.

images.altText: Microscopic view of thyroid follicular cells showing normal thyroid gland structure
images.caption: The thyroid gland produces hormones that regulate metabolism throughout the body.
images.credit: Histology of Thyroid gland / Wikimedia Commons

images.altText: Illustration showing the anatomical location of the thyroid gland in the neck
images.caption: The thyroid gland sits at the base of the neck, producing T3 and T4 hormones that regulate metabolism.

images.caption: The hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis: TRH from the hypothalamus stimulates TSH release from the pituitary, which stimulates thyroid hormone production.
